speaker: interviewer
text: After all you've been through--

speaker: interviewee
text: The hotel for my parents was such a mess and so dirty, so dirty that it was not such-- and then you find out everybody is killed and everybody was jealous, still jealous, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everything was on-- you couldn't buy anything just in the store. You have to get coupons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I went to get the card. So I came in the office and the card, and the man said come here. And I hear they're saying, here, the Jews are not back and they're going already in the front of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was a lot of-- distaste at the time what Hitler was in Holland, you know. And it was-- at first, of course, we valued freedom and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was very hard at first. When I came to the hotel, my brother had a child the same age as our baby. It was very hard for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was very hard. By the other hand, it was also we could afford staying at the hotel. Chaim and I, we builded it-- cleaned up the hotel, and we built it up, and-- and we did financially, later on, very well. And we could afford things, but-- and we got on two more children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are your other two children? They were born in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were born in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fred and Alida. And it was-- you know, I had nothing left in Holland. I remember I was very unhappy. Also, we didn't get any help from the Dutch government whatsoever. Chaim was Polish, and when you're in Holland-- and he's a businessman. And when you want to start in Holland a business, you have to go three years through study, for anything, for anything-- even when a restaurant opening, anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were lucky. My family was in the textile business, and so we got a license from one of the family what died in a concentration camp. And so we could open a store. Holland is a very-- there were no houses. You couldn't get anything to open where you could start a business. So he found somebody what had a store, and we had permission. So we opened a business there. And I know from the beginning that this is not very nice people. Chaim was desperate. And we didn't get along so good with my sister-in-law, and we lived together with one bathroom, living room, and everything was very primitive. And we lived on top of each other. And he had four children, and it didn't work out. So we went and started a business of our own. Of course, we didn't have any help from anybody else. And we want to go out of the hotel. And, finally, we made some money. We had to build a house.

speaker: interviewee
text: First, we had to buy a house, because the Dutch government didn't want to give us anything because we were not married before the war. And even when we came back to Holland, the head of the police said Chaim has to go back to Poland--

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because all the Polish people had to go back. So when we were in Holland, Chaim went hidden by some people in Amsterdam. And so I gave up my Dutch citizenship, and I say if Chaim goes, I want to go. If he goes to Poland, I go with him back to Poland, because I-- so it was very hard. We didn't have any love of-- any passion or anything in Holland in these years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you decided to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my whole family was killed-- my uncles, aunts, and everything. I had nothing left and just this brother, but we didn't get along with the sister-in-law. So I was a Zionist always. Chaim wanted to go to America. You couldn't come into America-- as Polish quota.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in Israel, you could go. So we sold and then-- oh, yeah. The partner we had a business with, he swindled us for everything-- for everything what we had. Only we came out with a little bit of money what we invested in it. So we sold everything in Holland-- everything-- and we went to Israel. Did you go to a kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to a [NON-ENGLISH]. The same idea as a kibbutz, only you live private. So we were at nine months, and my husband hated it. He couldn't have that kind of a life that everybody tells you what to do, and everybody says what to do. You have no own mind. You don't have to think about anything yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after nine months-- and he went every time, when he had a day off, he went looking for something. We went to [NON-ENGLISH], and he was a waiter. He was a waiter. And we went to a little village. Somebody from the kibbutz had a brother in a little village, Bet Yitzhak. And we went to Bet Yitzhak.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a village where everybody had to farm for themselves. And I worked in the field. I picked potatoes in the field, and I picked peppers. Also, in the kibbutz, was very hard work. I made bread at night. At 2 o'clock, I went up and made dough. I never baked in my life and made dough. And then, in the morning, at 6 o'clock, you have to make bread. And we went in the valley, we picked grapes and picked apples.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your children like the kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were small.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were young.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were 2 years, 3 years, and 5 years old. They don't know. Now, we were in this village. It was a very nice village and were more for well-to-do German-Jews lived there. And it was a nice group of people. And from the beginning we worked-- I-- I worked-- was labor, hard labor.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was a woman what had a vegetable store in Bet Yitzhak, for all the farmers around. And she went to America. And we had neighbors that were farmers, and they were very rich people from Holland-- very nice people. Not-- yeah, very-- but it has nothing to do with-- they were very nice people. And he said to Chaim, you have to buy that store. Chaim, no. How can I buy a store-- a vegetable store in a village? Everybody makes their own.

speaker: interviewee
text: Buy it. I give you the money. And, also, we were from the Anglo-Saxon in Israel. And they were very happy when they got people from Holland or from England, you know. So they were very nice to us. There was an organization-- a Dutch organization, I think, or an Anglo-Saxon. And they helped us, also gives us some money. And this man gave us some money. And we bought that vegetable store. And we could paid it off in the first month. Chaim was a very good businessman.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had-- in four villages, we went to Bet Yitzhak, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH]. In all the villages, every day, he went and met a friend of ours, and went together. And I stayed in the main business in Bet Yitzhak, and we sold vegetables to all the stores. So we did very well, but Chaim didn't like Israel. He had to go in the army right away, six weeks. And he had to go-- once a month, he had to go on-- Maneuvers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the town. And he didn't like that. And he-- he didn't like Israel. When we came to Israel, Chaim went to Japho. Japho, where all the Jewish immigrants what came out of concentration camps, and they went to Japho. And they lived a very primitive and small house. And I have an uncle on top of the Carmel. He was a doctor from Holland, and he came in 1946 to Israel-- '47, I think, he went. No, '47, he went to Israel, just before the state was there. And he lived have up the Carmel, in very nice house. I never came to there. And right away, the first part coming there was very beautiful. And Chaim was not so beautiful. So when he came back, says he, oh, how can we go back tomorrow to Holland. He didn't like it at all. So I think also the first impression for him was not so good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went-- we stayed with this family-- and then we went to Bet Yitzhak. And then we had the vegetable store, and we did very well. But it was very hard to get away from Israel, and you had to do it very secretive. Because when you went away from Israel, you was a traitor and nobody was to know about it. And you couldn't come into America. It was very hard. So neighbors from-- and we didn't have anybody in America-- nobody. We had a brother. My brother was in between, went to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- We didn't-- we couldn't come into Canada. I never-- we didn't think about it, going to Canada. We wanted to go to America. And we didn't have anybody in America-- no family, nobody. So-- so we had a lawyer-- we had papers. Oh, this uncle and aunt-- the doctor-- uncle and aunt, they went on visit into America. And they told the story to a lawyer in Buffalo for Chaim-- a Jewish lawyer. And he said, I don't like to do things like that, to take people out of Israel. But when I hear your story, I will give you an affidavit.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he gives an affidavit-- a stranger-- and so we had an affidavit. And now we had a lawyer in Washington. And he made the papers that we got quicker into America. A neighbor from us in Israel, they-- they went to America. And they went to Westport, Connecticut. So they told us, come to us. So we went with the boat. We went first to Italy, but we didn't want to go with plane, when it's better for the children that it's not so quick, you know. So we went to Italy. After Italy, we went on the boat to America. And we went to Westport, Connecticut. And that's the way we came to America. And then you moved to Branford.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we stayed 16 years. And we had a businesses in Stamford-- greeting card store. And then our daughter got married. And her husband was manager in a jewelry store. And we had enough of it-- 11 years in Stamford-- and we wanted to sell it, and we had a buyer for our store. And my-- the ex-- her ex-husband, what was at that time her husband, was in an jewelry store. And the man want to sell the jewelry store. So we bought a jewelry store, and went together with her husband. And that's the way-- that was in Old Saybrook. The store was in Old Saybrook. And that's the way we came to Branford, Connecticut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Selma, if you were to give advice to young people today about all your experiences in your life, what's the one lesson that-- from the Holocaust, that you would like them to understand and to know?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a very hard question. The hardest question is now for me that they're saying that there was no Holocaust. And that's the reason we go to schools and speaking in schools, because when I think, you know, where is my brothers, where are my mother? They never came back. All the family, the years stayed in Holland. Just have no prejudice. We're just all people. And we are the same. We're all the same, what color skin you have or what-- that makes no difference. Just-- we all want to live, and all want a family, and we want to be closer to our children. That always happens. And that's all that we want. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Being one of-- I think that there's only very few survivors left in the world from Sobibor, do think it's particularly important that teachers teach about resistance and about the uprising?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because that what I always tell also the kids in school. We were in Sobibor. We had a chance, because it was a very small camp, to steal food, even when we could be shot. But we did it anyway, because you don't care when you are in a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we didn't have concentration camp clothes on. We had regular shoes. And we were not so undernourished as the people from Auschwitz and Bergen-Belsen and all the other camps. And our camp was, in that time, was not big. So it almost couldn't happen in a camp like Auschwitz, only in a death camp. Like, they started in Belzec. In Belzec, they made an uprising, but they got caught. The people from Belzec came to Sobibor. They were killed in Sobibor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, also, the people from the Warsaw ghetto, but never was written up in any newspaper. They came to Sobibor-- the people what-- the people what were left in the Warsaw ghetto-- they were killed in Sobibor. So that was a death camp. That was not a working camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the camps were not that big. In this kind of camp, it could happen that way in an uprising. But in a camp like Auschwitz, and they had the striped clothes on, and their hair cut off, where could they go? Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where could they go? So that is what I always say--

speaker: interviewer
text: There were special circumstance in Sobibor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Special circumstances, that the Russian came. The Russians came and were out of military. And they came, and they gave us the courage. When that wouldn't have happened-- when they wouldn't have come, the plan would have been never there, and we never would have-- also, the men, what was the main person, what gived us the uprising in Sobibor-- I forgot his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sasha?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. A Jew, an inmate. His father was a rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tovi?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was all-- no, Tovi was just an inmate. He was the main upriser. Tovi was just an inmate like we are, but was very young when he was in camp. And his life is nothing else than Sobibor. So he-- he wrote-- he went with a writer to all over the world looking for people. And he is still-- that's all that he thinks about, is Sobibor. Now, in Washington, DC, is a memorial from the uprisings, and he is one of the organizers. And he's divorced, and he's-- he's very mixed up with that. No, Feldhendler was his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Feldhendler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Feldhendler was the main person from the uprising. And he went, after the war-- the war was over, he went to his hometown. And I don't know exactly what happened, but they shot him. Because I think that he was-- there non-Jewish people were afraid that they want to take his house, so somebody shot him. So-- and he was the main person. Alan Arkin played his role in the movie. And he was the main person what-- what was-- what was in the uprising-- and what was for the uprising. I don't think-- I don't know-- I'm not a scholar, and I'm not a scholar from the Holocaust, so I don't know. But I don't think an uprising could have just happened in small camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. When you speak to schools, can you see that children are eager to hear your story? Do you feel that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And especially because they saw the movie. And-- and, you know, they think, you know, like movie stars. And so they all connect that, I think, too. But they're very impressed. They're very impressed when we get tremendous-- before we're coming, they're very-- but when we are there, and we finish speaking to them, they're very impressed. And they write us-- they have to write essays for the teachers. But they're very impressive and very well-written, and-- and better than I can do it. Yeah. It makes it-- because the kids coming home and tell the parents, and, yeah, they are very impressed with when we tell. That's the reason-- one tells the other, and that's the reason we-- like the 50 years last year was too much for us to take, and we got both depression from it, because all the schools and everybody-- organizations, they want that we come and speak there, and it was a little bit too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your children and your grandchildren now. You have two children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two children. One son lives in Massachusetts, and he has two daughters. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your granddaughter's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Heidi and Emily. And Emily went to college in-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] maybe Washington? Maybe Washington? Anyway, she went just to college. And they're wonderful kids. Wonderful children, wonderful family. We don't see them much. They have very much their own life-- very much their own life. That's the reason we're very active in speaking in schools, so that we don't feel so much that the kids have so much their own life. And then we have a daughter--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now she's in New Haven.

speaker: interviewee
text: In New Haven. We see her more, and we are very close to these children. One just graduated Sarah Lawrence, and she is now in New York, and has a hard time finding a job and doing something. And then, other one is in college in Massachusetts. She is in Amherst College-- no, she is in-- oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Blank on the moment. Really, Chaim has to sit here and he can tell me. She is in college now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.

speaker: interviewee
text: Great kids. All four are great kids. And our children do financially well. They're working hard and they do well.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's great. And, in one minute, we'll hear from Chaim.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you will hear from Chaim. This is a photo from-- from my parents when they had to-- when they builded a hotel. And that is all Jewish people what sitting here. Only not the waiters, perhaps, like this waiter. And the hotel was not finished. And it was in 1932, when they started building the hotel. This the hotel from my parents. This is my maiden name. And that was the hotel that they built. We could show before it was the tent, and it was a hotel. It was a kosher hotel. It was the only kosher hotel in state of-- in the Province of Overijssel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is also a part of the hotel, on the other side. The hotel is now very neglected, and it is not so nice anymore. In that time, it was a very nice and new hotel. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture from when we were very small, from-- that is my brother, what died in Auschwitz. And this is my brother what died together with him. And that's me. I didn't die yet. I was-- I was 7 years old or something like that. This is a picture from my brothers and me of my mother's 50th birthday. So that was in year 1939. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my brother, Marthyn. This is my brother, Mauritz. And that's me, and that my brother, Abraham, what made through the war. He was hidden. These two both died in Auschwitz. This is a picture from me. I was 18 years old. This is my brother, what was a dancing teacher, and that's me. And, in Holland, when-- when you-- when there's a party, and there's dancing, a ball or something like that, then you have to have somebody what has to study-- he really studied for that, to give the leading on it. So-- and he gave dancing lessons. And so I danced with him and dancing lessons. This is our marriage license. We were in Poland, and we went to the town hall, and we had some witnesses on there. The name is on there. And that was our marriage license. And this is same. That's for Chaim's. That's Chaim's marriage license. And it was-- I don't know-- it was in a very small town in Poland. Chaim. This is a picture from Chaim and me and our first baby, Emilchen, in-- in Poland. We lived in Poland, in [NON-ENGLISH], in that time. In this, the picture in here, in Branford made.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a couple what lived in Israel. Can you make that? This is Eda and Yitzhak. And they're also survivors from Sobibor. Secrets.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. This is a picture that was made in Odessa when we were on the way to Holland. And this is the little baby, our baby, and that's me. And there is Chaim somewhere, too, here. And there is Oolah. Oolah was also a survivor from Sobibor, and she was also one of the Dutch at camp. She died very young. She was 50 when she died in Israel. This is a picture for Chaim. He was in the Polish Army. And that's Chaim. He had a very difficult time in the Polish Army. Very difficult. He was-- really suffered. That is our son, Fred, and that is Alida. Liddy, we call her. And that's before we went to Israel. That is a picture made before we immigrated to Israel. This is a picture from Ellis and Bob Gwilym. And they played, in the movie, from and they fell in love, and they got married and have two little girl-- no, a boy and a girl. And they live in London, and are very happy. Very nice. The first picture, this is my husband with our daughter-in-law, Barbara Engel. And this-- and the second picture is our daughter, and this is Kendra, and there is Tagan. That's our two grandchildren. And the other picture is also Liddy, our daughter, with Kendra and Tagan. They're now 21 and 19. And the other picture is our son, Fred, and Chaim in our kitchen.
